interview.a: Clean melodic Chill-Out

He started creating his own music when he joined audiotool.com. Before that he had played cover songs on the piano.
2011 via Google appstore. Attracted by RNZR's remix of "When stars collide" by Hbeao.

For me, the most important part of making music, is having fun. Audiotool makes this effortless with its intuitive and straightforward layout. With the amazing sample browser at hand and a few simple effects, its easy to make my creativity to flow. Without Audiotool I would probably never have started producing any music or even continued to play an instrument (sorry if this sounds like a bad commercial, but I mean it). For the community i just want to say: Make the music you want to make, don't make the music everyone else wants you to make. Good artists evolve, when they follow their heart, think outside the box and experiment. New sounds and concepts can evolve from simple ideas and this is what makes music such an amazing thing.

Have fun and stay humble. Cheers Zone.

Digital audio workstations (DAWs) exist to assist in the making of music. But that doesn't mean they are easy ways to escape hard work. A DAW is like any other instrument; you must practice in order to gain experience and get better. If you find that your work isn't up to your standards, then familiarity with your tools is more important than more tools. After all, you don't know what that knob does until you twist it, so you should twist it before you get more knobs. So my advice for Audiotoolers is this: if you want to make better music, then eat more peaches.

intro: Martin Below aka mbelow is a powerful force in the Audiotool team. His special field is complex data structures, which he processes with the dynamic drive with which he also approaches drumming in his metal band. A lot of our great innovations are largely due to his unbending will to optimize. Share with us the pleasure of having him on board!
about:
Name: Martin Below
Place: Cologne (Germany)
Age: 34

interview.q: What is your job at Audiotool?
interview.a: I joined audiotool about 1 1/2 years ago and took over Joa's duties on website and backend development. As some of you know, Joa is now focusing on a cross-platform development toolkit called defrac, which we use to build the html5 version of audiotool. Now after the recent relaunch of the audiotool website, I started working with Andre on the App.

interview.q: What is your educational background for the job you are doing?
interview.a: I started studying computer science but dropped it shortly after. Then, I worked as a freelance software developer for different companies in the industry. Oh, and I also studied the drumset, which I think is what qualifies me for my job at audiotool in the first place.

interview.q: When and how did you start programming?
interview.a: I got my first computer in the early nineties (an IBM PC XT with 640KB RAM running at 4.77 MHz). Thank you, mom & dad. At that time, this machine was already outdated and in general not such a good fit for gaming. Well, it could run some simple, mostly text-based games, but all the cool guys in school already had their Amigas and Commodores, which where way ahead in that department. So I started figuring out what else could be done with a computer, and finally looked into this BASIC interpreter thing that was used for running most of these simple games. I think I started just by blindly modifying existing sources and observing the effect of the changes.

interview.q: How would you define your musical style?
interview.a: I have have only made my first few tracks so far, but I think they would fit best in the ambient drawer.
Metal
I started playing drums at the age of 12 and played mostly in thrash- and death metal bands. I was good enough to impress some girls with it, of which one eventually became my wife.

Keep on making this great music on Audiotool! And please be patient. We know that very little has been added to the app in the last months, but there is a lot of stuff going on under the hood. Andre already has a bunch of great ideas for new tools and toys that will be integrated as soon as we have the full-fledged application running on html5. Stay tuned!

Find out for yourselves.
about:
Name: Liam Robertson
Place: Glasgow, Scotland (UK) "I lived in Minnesota, USA until moving to the UK at age nine"
Age: 13
Profession: Williamwood High School

interview.a: I don't really like to limit myself to a particular style or genre, but what I make is usually pretty chilled out. Other than that, I don't know what I'd call it. It's often focused on synths, chords and melodies, but sometimes I go through future garage stages where percussion is the main thing.

I've always been into piano. Even before I started getting lessons at the age of six, I'd play around, making up little pieces. I've always preferred composing small songs on the piano over playing pieces by other people, and piano is still a major part in making my tracks.
Although I'd been making music on the piano many years before, I only started making music electronically earlier this year.

I actually found audiotool well over a year ago in search of an easy way to make music. This was when I really started to get into lots of different types of music - and I really wanted to try it out for myself. I gave up after a week. Everything I tried to do just ended up turning into trash, and so I forgot about audiotool for a long time, until about half a year ago - something brought me back to the site, and some of the stuff I was hearing was really impressive. I decided to give it another shot, and for the past 5 months I've been hooked.

Audiotool is the coolest way to get into making music. The relatively small community makes everyone's presence so much more valuable - so make the music you love. Audiotool is where you can get away with that. By making the music you want to hear, you will end up with a group of people who love your music, which is what we all need as artists starting out on Audiotool.

intro: With his authentic Retro style Jordi Moragues has secured himself a firm place among the popular Audiotool artists. Crystal clear synth melodies reminiscent of forerunners of electronic music like Vangelis and Jean Michel Jarre. Check him out and feel the spirit of those pioneer days.
about:
Name: Jordi Moragues Massanet
Place: Barcelona, Spain. Currently I live in Cologne, Germany
Age: 44
Profession: Currently dance teacher. Before that I was a computer animator. Studied and worked at the University of the Balearic Islands and the Academy of Media Arts in Cologne.

interview.a: Classic, or Retro, highly influenced by the popularization era of electronic music in the late 70s and early 80s and artists like Vangelis, Jarre or Kraftwerk.

I don't have formal training, have always been self-taught, but recently I have started to study and educate myself in music theory on my own. I also don't have any professional experience, apart from making the soundtrack of most of my old computer animation shorts. Making music has always been a hobby for me.
In the early 90s at home, with a Roland W-30, to which later I added a few more instruments from Roland and Korg.

Just by chance when it was still part of Hobnox, some time around 2008, looking for music-making online programs. I was curious but did not stick to it. I rediscovered it at the end of 2012 as Audiotool.com and have been hooked to it ever since.

Thanks a lot to Andre and his team for this fantastic platform. I am constantly learning with it and don't think I will ever feel limited. I am looking forward to their new ideas for the upcoming version. I am also amazed at the talent and the high quality of the music I have found in this community. I encourage audiotoolers to keep creating and experimenting. Research and listen to lots of music, learn the basics of music theory, copy and understand the techniques from the pros, open and analyse tracks you like to understand what makes them tick, experiment a lot, know each device inside out, get creative, try unusual arrangements and don't feel pressed to publish too often.

intro: To reduce Nionsomnia to one style would be a sin. Over and over again he has demonstrated his command of Dubstep, Hip Hop - including singing his own Raps - and other variations of Bass Music. But he also produces heart-rending Trance synths and even banging Hardstyle Beats with an authenticity of style that would suggest a much more experienced producer than he can be at his tender age.
about:
Name: Nionwa Jahi (Nee-ahn-wa) (Ya-hee)
Place: Riverside, California (USA)
Age: 17
Profession: Summerville Highschool. I'm studying electricity.

interview.a: I make all types of music as long as it displays feeling or a good message. I honestly just make whatever mood I'm in at the moment. Any song I make comes from what the times have called for. What the movement of spirit has called for. So I would define my style as... movement. And feeling. I hardly ever know what genre I'm going to end up with at the end of the day.

My whole family has always been artistic in some way. Whether it be speaking, writing, cooking, martial arts or playing an instrument. I've always been surrounded by instruments and have always banged on drums, but took it seriously when I got my drum set at 10 years old. Everyday down in the basement I just devoted my life to playing music, and progressively, I got better and better. It was never something that I planned out. It was also never really something I got lessons for. It's not like I was against lessons, but looking back I guess I was self-assured so that whatever the preferred sound it came from my head, onto the drums, and out to the universe and was meant to sound like that. If I had taken lessons it would have just sounded like the preferred sound in the instructor's head that they could say was proper, but not genuine for me. From the drums came guitars, and keyboards, and little cheap radios that had cassettes in them that my brother and I would attempt to record professional-sounding rock songs on (yeah right). But at the time it was golden, because the theme of it was creation. And creation is life. From then my love for creating grew and in my freshmen year in high school my friend introduced me to audiotool. For a while I would just listen to all the artists on there while doing school work, just wondering how in the world they made all those amazing sounds. One day, I clicked the "APP" button and literally just started creating. Knowing real instruments I guess it was a plus when I saw all the guitar looking pedals that I later found were plugins for effects. And from there, I've just been learning more and more about electronic music, or even making music in general.
I started making music at 10.
My friend showed me the site. He said he didn't really know how to use it and that I should check it out. So I did.

I always felt love for all the artists on here that devote their time to doing what they love the most, which is making music. And the ones who are into vibing with the times and are open to a positive change. On your journey, have fun and learn from people, but be your own biggest critic. Don't get hung up so much on what someone else has to say, because then you are no longer going off the music that comes from your soul, but the sound that someone else will forget about the next day. Embrace self and things will flow. Because what matters more: the name, or the character behind the name?

tracks.name: Phoenix ft. Aaron - Trying to be cool
Dirty Dan (Dubstep)
tracks.desc: Some dirty dirty dub. Enough said. The creepy feeling the beginning has that leads up to the drop is so sudden that it intensifies everything. This is what got me making scary sounding dub for a while.

tracks.name: Soundwaves
tracks.desc: Almate and I kind of rose up together. I’m happy he’s improved so much. This song actually got me into experimenting with dnb production. My first attempt was when I remixed Soundwaves. It was a pretty big success for me.

tracks.name: Chill Penguins
tracks.desc: This is a really glitchy track in a good way. ABstract always seems to maintain a pop in his songs. This inspired me to write Time Bomb, which made top 100 in Moogfest.

tracks.name: NXS#_004
tracks.desc: When I heard a few of Solace's hits, it really made me question my dnb skills in general. He's got drum and bass on lock. After a while I started getting ideas from him and ended up getting better at making dnb.

tracks.name: Make Me Feel
tracks.desc: I have a deep appreciation for classic house. The 80s-sounding sample really tied this song together. This proved to me that any genre is possible on Audiotool.

tracks.name: Cherry Blossom Road
tracks.desc: It’s all in the strings in this song. The violins set the mood for the track and the percussion somehow fits right in with it. Offbeat’s usage of chopped samples in instrumental hip hop really reminds me of nujabes and has inspired me to try more of that type of stuff. Now I can never get enough of digging through records.

tracks.name: Meteor Flare
tracks.desc: In my opinion one of the very best constructed and best sounding dubstep songs on Audiotool. This inspired me to write Pyrostepping.

intro: Cripta aka Marco Falcini is the Audiotool Techno producer of the first hour and creator of the legendary quote "No Pulve no party". Hard to believe that he brought with him no previous skills in music production. Always stylistically confident, always authentic he delivers one dance-floor filler after another.
about:
Name: Marco Falcini
Place: Livorno (Tuscany, Italy)
Age: 35
Profession: Currently unemployed (thanks Italy, love you so much) what's school ?? :)

interview.a:
It depends on the inspiration of the moment. But yes, most of my tracks are Techno-house and derivates.

If i still had a guitar and a bass, I would definitely make more Rock 'n Roll :)
Piano, bass, guitar, drums, then DJing and producing. Everything autodidact and just for fun. That's why I suck at them all ;)
I started messing around with electronic music softwares 5-6 years ago, with Ableton and Reason, then Audiotool.

I was looking for an online music maker program and found Audiotool browsing the internet.

It was still called Hobnox. But I never used it because I couldn't. I published my first track when it had already entered the Audiotool era, after some months testing and studying the software.

For audiotoolers: NO PULVE NO PARTY (of course)

For the staff: well, you are all great people, and the site is great. Wish I was a software programmer born in Cologne, so I could help you in some way. And can't wait for the new shit.

tracks.name: path II
tracks.desc: Olondro has his definite and recognizable style, always original and never obvious or boring. I love a lot of Olondro tracks,it was really hard to choose only one. But probably this is my favourite.... TRICKY !!

tracks.name: Cubrick
tracks.desc: He came on Audiotool and boom! A new world of sound! GENIUS!!!

tracks.name: Abducted
tracks.desc: Probably the AT artist closest to my taste. His music is a sexy underground club. It was hard too choose only one track cause I love them all. But this is maybe my favourite.

tracks.name: Soundlab
tracks.desc: He's another one of my greatest inspirations. I could not believe his tracks were made on Audiotool, so professional. I learnt everything opening and spying his tracks. And this track is one of my favourites of his. But eh... missing any of Chunk tracks is a crime. MASTER OF SYNTHESIS!!

tracks.name: Pointless Tune
tracks.desc: EliaTrix is another pioneer of the

tracks.name: Corner Shop
tracks.desc: Open this track. You won't believe your eyes. Ascent is simply a fantastic producer with very good taste, too. What else ? For me a point of reference... TOP !!!

intro: With JamBam and Retro you're not talking about bombastic Synthpop ballads or tempestuous cow-bells. Without a doubt his style was shaped by the club sounds of the early 1990s. Be it Acid, Trance or the raw Drum and Bass sounds of the end of the century, he's got them all at his fingertips and has only to add that distinctive JamBam something. His track "Set phazers on everything" made it onto the AT Cassette for the Moogfest 2014 from more than 1,000 applicants.
about:
Name: Jamie Cooling
Place: The U.K. originally form Canterbury in Kent but I've lived in London long enough to have a cockney accent :)
Age: 26, definitely 26.............. I am absolutely not any older than 26........... ok I'm 35
Profession: Alcoholic beverage intake management consultant (barman)

interview.a: I have no idea what I'm going to make when I open the app but I would have to say I'm predominantly about acid. I love everything 303, seriously if I was from Texas I probably would have married one by now. I dabble in ambient, trance and housey stuff too and I've developed a real love of making dnb. At the moment I've been listening to a lot of hard glitched up stuff like square pusher and there's lots of crazy stuff coming out of Japan (isn't there always?). It's tough to do and takes a long time but I reckon it's a direction I would like to go in, so long as I can fit a 303 in it somewhere!

I haven't the foggiest on how to play anything! I did used to carry around a guitar but that was just to get girls. In the 90's my older brother was in a DJ collective called 'Cloud Nine'. Through them I got into the clubbing and free party scene and I quickly developed a love for everything about it. The gigs, the atmosphere, the people you met and all this amazing new music like acid, techno, trance, hardcore, dnb etc. Then working in bars pubs and clubs I was always surrounded by music and I always wanted to try my hand at producing. But on a barmans wage there was no way I could afford all the gear needed. Then I found Audiotool...
On Audiotool, but if you count air guitaring to Nirvana - 1993

About three years ago I think, I was messing about on StumbleUpon and Andre's Tonematrix popped up. Before I knew it I'd been sat there for about half an hour playing around, I noticed the banner saying "Check out my day job" clicked on it and there it was, this amazing site full of great music and cool people!

My mind gets blown by the music on Audiotool everyday. Keep doing it! This site is just brilliant, put together with dedication and passion by the devs and populated by an awesome (if slightly disfunctional) family ;) It means someone like me (a guy that didn't even know what a sine wave was) can make music and even get featured. Oh and on that note - YEAH BITCHES I GOT FEATURED! HAHA!!!!!!! I'M OFF TO MARRY A KARDASHIAN! THE WORLD NEEDS MORE JAMBAM!

tracks.name: destroyed me
tracks.desc: If Nom got me started it was Torn that got me going, the help and encouragement was invaluable and it made me stay on AT. This track is definitely my fave of his - that robot voice, the breaks with the melancholy pads, beautiful. Makes me want to buy the guy a pint and listen to his story.

tracks.name: REmix II (Ksvbi)
tracks.desc: Ah Tott, you know the guy is so chilled and laid back I'm pretty sure he's a rastafarian, and that mellow attitude is relflected in his music. Hehe na he is the master of dark dnb and to me this is his best, that grimey bass hitting you in waves with the amen on top, class! Plus this is the guy that got me into making dnb, cheers dude you're a legend!

tracks.name: ...and then all this crazy-ass robots appeared
tracks.desc: I have this as my ringtone :) I opened this up had a look and learned tons, so simple and yet the sound design is so good, the beats, those little blips, that base and that title..... totally weird, totally brilliant.

tracks.name: A Certain Kind of Bullet
tracks.desc: And now we're getting into the 303 side of things! This is the kind of music I love in clubs; hard, melodic and with a gritty acid line going through it. Check out the 303 work at 02:42, then the drop at 03:30 I'd be going nuts at that bit!

tracks.name: The Simpleton (2014)
tracks.desc: A seriously talented dude who was a massive help early on in AT, he even took the time to make me a template explaining compression, truely

tracks.name: Jalousie
tracks.desc: I wish I could make music like this, my god he's good at doing glitched up percs and beats! And when the vocals come in..... yeah. This guy deserves way more faves than he gets, I literally have an album of his stuff on itunes that'll take me a day to listen to, get on his page and get listening!

Again if I'm making a list Inavon has got to be in it. In my opinion he's the best at making trance on AT, his arps and pads are always spot on and then mixing in the 303 - perfect, I'd be very happy bouncing along to this on a beach in Goa!

intro: Whole-hearted musician and number three of the most popular Audiotool artists. Patrick White aka Uprising comes up with professional sounding Bass-Music of all categories and thus serves as a model for many of our up and coming producers. It's only his output rate lately that gives any cause for complaint. Come on, Up!
about:
Name: Patrick White
Place: Tulsa Oklahoma (USA)
Age: 38
Profession: Sous-chef in a fine

interview.a: I like to make DnB, Dubstep, Hip hop, Chill, Classical and Experimental. I also like to try to make pretty much anything I think would be fun to do.

I original started my journey into music when I was about 8 years old with the violin. I played violin all the way through school and still play whenever I get a chance.

After school I played Guitar in a Death Metal band called Deadfall. We had lots and lots of fun. After Deadfall, I played bass in a band called Plexity in Houston TX. We had some mild successes and a lot of fun.

I didn't start making music on the Computer until I discovered Audiotool.

I knew I wanted to do something with the computer and music so I searched for apps that I could play with for fun and quickly found Audiotool.

At first, I thought Audio tool was a neat little toy so played around with sampling and the beat machines and published it.

This is when I found out that this new toy of mine had a whole community attached to it.

I remember it well. It was Tornsage who first commented on and faved my track. Then he followed me. This made me feel real good, so I did what any respectful new Audiotooler would do and spammed my crappy little sampled track all over creation and back. This is when I got the best advice of my life on AT. It was left in a comment by a few different people. It read something like this, "Stop spamming your tracks all over the place and learn to use the Pulverisateur. If you do this and participate in the community people will find you!"

I took this advice and started listening to tracks and I quickly found that Audiotool can do so much more then I had originally thought. I started to love the music I was hearing and talking to people who were way more active than I ever anticipated people being on a little music toy that I found in the google app store.

This is When Audiotool Stopped being a just toy for me and became a way of life and a place to hang and make music with people I respect and care about. I am now hooked for life.

You guys are amazing. It is a pleasure be a part of all the talent and musical originality that is Audiotool. The support we have for each other cannot be found on any other website in the universe and for that I thank you. Audiotool is where all the cool kids hang and I am glad to be a part of it.

intro: For some time we can observe a steady increase in Trap producers and tracks. One of the driving forces behind this development is B-ST from Senegal. Never denying his Hip Hop roots, he produces one mature Trap beat after another. Definitely worth checking out!
about:
Name: Bocar Talla
Place: Senegal, but currently live in the US
Age: 17
Profession: Music Producer
School: Southwick-Tolland-Granville Regional High School (S.T.G.R.H.S)

interview.a: Especially with my latest beats, I would say that my style is somewhat cinematic yet can very easily be described as Hip Hop or Trap depending the genre I'm going for. I usually just go with what I feel like works the best when making a track (but obviously with some foundation).

From a younger age, music has always been in my heart. As a kid, I would always tap to the beat of any song I heard. My brother used to rap and I would always watch him and his friends playing with FL Studio and recording. My dad sings and plays the guitar, keyboard, and drums. When I was 11, my dad (who sings and plays guitar, keyboard and drums) taught me the c scale and from them on I tough myself how to play keyboard/piano from videos on YouTube.

Around age 11-12 the online game Fantasy DJ wad what started me up in music production. The first program I used was Soundation, then I moved to ClubCreate, and later found Audiotool; now I produce beats using FL Studio as well.

At the time when ClubCreate was gradually fading from the internet, I started to search for other free web-based studios I could make music on, and so I found Audiotool; which I immediately left because it was so foreign to me and I had no idea how it worked. A month later I came back and stepped into a whole new world and embarked on the journey that brought me where I am now.

To the people just starting Audiotool or making music itself, making music may not come easily to all of us, but that's no reason why we can't be as good as we want to be. There's nothing wrong with sampling; some of the best music out there has samples. If you're just starting out, don't be afraid to experiment with samples and see how different, yet relevant you can make the track. Force yourselves to improve and try new things. Curiosity can help you reach the pinnacles you plan to reach. Remember, Audiotool is a powerful tool... use it to your advantage.

tracks.name: "Another life" Vocals by KrazieKammie (rec in AT)
Fresh Beatz
tracks.desc: As soon as I heard this I opened it up to study how Infy made this

tracks.name: Bass Up
tracks.desc: This is definitely one of my all time AT favorites. Everyday, I strive to make EDM Trap tracks as epic as this one as well as some other tracks on Aodiotool. The drop rendered me speechless. I don't think I could easily get sick of listening to this.

tracks.name: Solaris (feat. Luxior)
tracks.desc: What I love about this song is how chill yet powerful it sounds. The chord progression and melody were excellent. The work that was put into this track definitely shows.

tracks.name: Time Is Candy (with Ignapon3, Luxior, Almate, Teqtoniq and Kavil)
tracks.desc: When I saw the title of this track, I knew it was going to be great; it wasn't what I expected when I first played it, but I fell in love with it. Needless to say, the track was very well produced, but my favorite part, the part that kills me is the drop tag; love it!

tracks.name: Sole Survivor
tracks.desc: The dark atmosphere of the track in the intro just keeps you suspended and waiting to see what happens. I loved the live-sounding drums, the chord pregression, and how the track was arranged; it just moved me.

tracks.name: rabbit
tracks.desc: The serenity of this track captured my attention. I'm a big fan of piano tracks and calm/chill tracks so this one was the best of both worlds for me.

intro: In the person of Alain Groetzinger we have a real piece of music history in our midst. In the 70ies, as the drummer of the french Art-Rock band "Rockets" he sold millions of records. Today he inspires us with his banging Electro-Pop anthems full of soul and humanistic ideals. This week his autobiographical book "The Dream of Metal" is going to be released. One more reason for us to take a closer look at him. So should you too.
about:
Name: Alain Groetzinger
Place: I lived in Paris until 2013 and now I am in Britanny, in a small village.
Age: 59
Profession: Since 2007 I've been working for an animal welfare organization. I take care of everything related to advertising, newspapers, the Internet, all written and visual communication aspects.

interview.a: Hmm... I'm not sure I've got one. Overall, I like to switch between styles like Rock, House, Electro-Space and Downtempo. But I also like Athmosphere. It is wide but I love this diversification. But also, I almost always try to say something fundamentally human with voices which themselves are not at all human. It's sort of my gimmick.

I started playing the drums when I was 10 years old. The drum became my great friend throughout my adolescence. It was like a sort of permanent dream. Then I had the good luck to meet the band members of "Rockets" and I became their drummer. Yet another step in the dream. I was 19 years old. But in addition, the group bacame the number one foreign group in Italy. We sold more than a million and a half records. The dream became "The Dream of Metal." This name comes from the fact that we all had a metallic appearance, and all our instruments were metal. "The Dream of Metal" is also the name of the book that I have written, in which I tell the hole story. I hope they will do an english version too.

Then I went through a long period of immersion in Experimental music, noisy minimalistic, Electro. I called it "music to fall asleep". It was a real soul searching. I could spend hours listening to the sound of a drop of water or the noise of the wind. My delight was to play other sounds, one after another, without hearing the transition. Each sequence could take several minutes. But it was an amazing experience. If you try this exercise with the song of a bird and a hammer for example...

In 2011, after this great meditative period, I felt a great need to think of music from a different point of view: that of SHARING. Radically opposite to what I have lived for for 10 years. So in looking for a new opening, so a new adventure, I arrived on Audiotool. When I discovered all the possibilities of expression and sharing that this site provided, it was like a new artistic birth. For me, despite what I knew, it was just magical, revolutionary. It is still my view today.

My first message is to express my appreciation for all Audiotool musicians whatsoever whether they are famous or not. Among these artists ther are many references for me. Basically, what I often see is exactly what we do not hear: it is a kind of vibration that the musician allows to flow into his music without even realizing it. That's what I love. That's the magic of music. That's why I like certain artists, even if they do not have the professionality of some others. This, to me, is not of great importance.

My second message is to reaffirm the essential role of Audiotool for us all. But also the mind, the feeling, the openness and all the hard work of the team to ensure that Audiotool is such a possibility of expression. This, I think every time I open the application to find new music...

tracks.name: three minutes after the world died
Under The Same Sun
tracks.desc: Olaf is the first artist whose atmospheric music touched me. I have always followed him and paid attention to his creations which are always of very good taste.

tracks.name: Planes
tracks.desc: Julsy lies halfway between the very quiet music and the faster, more rhythmic tracks. But it's always in a Zen spirit which I like so much.

tracks.name: Stellar magnetic field
tracks.desc: It's Daftwill's frank to and fro between rhythmic and

tracks.name: Emancipation
tracks.desc: With Cihangir I glide with delight in the sensual and hot music into the depth of night. His music has always appeared exceptionally pure, both in terms of the spirit of its sounds, its arrangements ... well, for me it is a must.

tracks.name: autoscontro
tracks.desc: Music composed by Cripta will serve me to build a bridge to the next track of this album. His style is also very wide-ranging, this is what I like. He always maintains an excellent musical taste, which in my opinion places him among the best sound designers of Audiotool. For me, this is a must-musician.

tracks.name: Rise
tracks.desc: So Jambosh is the one who I have always felt close to. I mean the style, sound etc. I've always loved the high quality of his tracks but also and above all the energy that is always there. This boy has always fired Audiotool with positive vibrations. Another recommendation.

intro:
Oedipax and Jonjon, both AT legends of the first hour, demonstrate with their story how Audiotool can bring people together accross borders. Of course mostly this happens on a musical level. In this special case it led to more: Our congratulations go to Suzanne and Jonjon, who married in June 2014. We are proud to introduce to you the first intercontinental Audiotool couple.

Not forgetting that independently of one another they have played an outstanding role in the qualitative development of Audiotool music over the years.
about:
Oedipax
Name: Suzanne Greninger
Place: Seattle, Washington, USA
Age: 35
Profession: Administrative Manager at a nonprofit

Jonjon
Name: Jon Clark
Place: Seattle, U.S. (Originally from Coventry, England)
Age: 31
Profession: Self Employed Driver

interview.a:
Oedipax: I started learning classical piano at the age of 10 and have been playing ever since, occasionally branching off into some modern music.

Jonjon: I've never learned an instrument, but I tinkered with drums, lots of keyboards and a couple of synths as a kid. I grew up with lots of Reggae and Ska around through my father. Then one day, as a 10 year old, he bought me the Jungle-Mania cassette tapes and I was hooked! I would listen to the old Rave tapes at school, then I took up the turntables around 15, I taught myself how to mix and scratch records and spent much of my youth at record shops, club nights, raves and so on. I did a few DJ sets with my mate but nothing much. I haven't mixed a single record for over 5 years. Maybe I'll treat myself to some 1210's and start collecting old records again.

Oedipax: Right now, it's primarily dark and influenced by Industrial and technical/mechanical sounds and falling a lot within what people would call Drum and Bass or Jungle. I also dabble a bit with Jazz samples within a Jungle or Drum and Bass context.

Jonjon: Hard for me to say, but I believe in good drums that still have that older more acoustic sound and I love to pair a deep low end bass with the beat. I'll usually have some theme going and if I'm in the mood, I'll add a half decent melody.

Oedipax: I started regularly composing melodies and writing compositions on the piano at the age of 13, however, I found Audiotool in 2011 and started making electronic music at the ripe old age of 31! ;)

Jonjon: I was 18 years old and mostly used Reason

Oedipax: I found Audiotool through I believe what was Google's app store at the time and was introduced to electronic music production through this handy platform in early 2011.

Jonjon: In 2009 Luke recommended the original Hobnox site to me, so I uploaded some music and was actually really blown away by some of the musicians and video artists on there. Then I signed up for the first version of audiotool the night it went live. It was a few weeks before I published "Man in Grey" which was part of all those early tracks that I deleted.

Oedipax: Jonjon found me! ;) I saw that he faved one of my tracks on Audiotool and said something nice about it back in 2011. I clicked on his name, listened to a couple of his tracks and realized his great talent straight away. We started talking about music and both found common ground through our similar interests. Over the course of a year, we wrote about music, shared our thoughts about art, life, and many other topics, and learned from each other. Eventually, we decided we needed to meet in person. John came to the U.S. last year and stayed with me, and I found upon meeting him, it was like meeting someone I had already known forever. Before he had to go back to the UK, he proposed. I accepted wholeheartedly. Through the process of U.S. immigration and loads of research and paperwork on our part, he eventually was able to make it back here to the U.S. in May of this year. We married in June and we are living very happily together. :)

Jonjon: Warning: Have tissues at the ready. I was drawn to Suzanne from the moment I heard her early experimental tracks such as "Burn" and she was really into my afterglow remix. We began chatting on each others walls, then through email, video, pics, phone. Eventually we found we had written a huge romantic novel through e-mail! We share much in common and had experienced similar periods in life. We became best friends. Then we became romantic and I went over to Seattle a couple of times for the maximum period of time allowed. We got to know each other, we fell in love and had a lot of fun. I proposed to her at the beach one summer's evening, Suzanne accepted and then a week later, through many tears, I had to depart back to England and begin the whole immigration process which took an age, mountains and mountains of paperwork and much longing. Suzanne came to England for the first time to meet my family. When I got approved to return, I was the happiest man on earth. So then I went back for good, we married in June this year and we're enjoying life together very much.

Oedipax: Thank you all for all the music you make. Many of you are coming up with such innovative and incredible music. Keep doing what you do for the happiness you find in it. Many of you also know how much making music can be used as a creative and even personal outlet for emotions, dreams, and thoughts. Take it as an opportunity for exploration whether you want to express a deeper part of yourself or simply use it as an opportunity to learn more about certain aspects of music production. Whether you're just starting out or have been at it for years, remember that what you have within you is priceless regardless of popularity, fame, or recognition. Keep learning with fresh eyes and listening with fresh ears, but mostly, don't forget to have fun. ;)

intro: We close 2014 with the remarkebly talanted Cally Kay from Ohio US. As one of the few female users she has found herself a place among the well known Audiotool artists. We would like to take this opportunity to encourage more female musicians to join our community in 2015. Come on girls!
about:
Caroline Kay Kennedy
Place: Ohio, United States of America
Age: 18
University: Business Marketing and Technology

interview.a: I've been singing ever since I could speak and at the age of 6 I took my first piano class for a year. I taught myself until I was 13 and then started back up taking classes from a professional pianist. My family is oriented around music. My father is teaching my younger brother to play the drums, my sister is teaching herself to play guitar by ear and she, my mother and I all sing. I am also currently taking piano lessons today and I recently had the opportunity to sing at a Veterans' Day Assembly with an audience of 1,300 people. On a normal basis, I lead the congregational music at my local church every Sunday and Wednesday Evening.

I would define my style as Trance, Techno, easy to listen to and danceable. Oh! And always optimistic. I have tried to make sad or evil sounding music and I just fail miserably. I also compare some of my tracks' styles to Owl City. They are always peppy, happy and encouraging. I make music to make people's day.
I did not start making music electronically until I found Audiotool. Before that I would compose music on my keyboard for leisure.

It was a cold and wet April Evening of my freshman year in high-school, so 3-4 years ago. I had recently downloaded Google Chrome because it was all the rage! I was playing around with the different music applications and came across Audiotool. It was everything I wanted and more. The community came together and helped me with almost anything I needed. People like Uprising, Tornsage, ZOD4X, Smily, and Inavon supported me and helped promote my music without me even having to ask them to. This has been the most brilliant experience and I never want it to end.

Thank you Audiotool, all the staff and producers and literally everyone who took part in helping make this site possible and running as smoothly as it does. I am so thankful for this, it is my number one hobby and favorite passtime. This site has changed my outlook on music and has helped me to discover what I would like to do in the future. As for the Community... Thank you to all my followers, fans, listeners and even to the haters! Your input helps me to be not only a better musician but even a better person in general. Thank you to the many more who like my me and my music and are supporting me. My advice to you is be creative, think outside the box, research, ask for help, experiment, be Optimistic, Love Chicken and <3 Audiotool.

tracks.name: Surface
tracks.desc: Jake Tyler OH MY GOSH!!! His music is always amazing. It's full of feeling and emotion which is always key when producing music. This song specifically I listen to when I am angry or like when I need to get something done. He makes the most beautiful music, filled with passion which is clearly seen in all his work. Every song he makes just keeps getting better and better.

tracks.name: The Knot at the Rope's End
tracks.desc: I mean come on, it's Inavon. I like reflecting my styles off of his in general but this song is one of my all time favorites by him. This song reminded me of the 80’s trance and I love that era of music. Me and my Dad listen to this a bunch when we drive.

interview.a: PIZZA ... normally I (re)mix everything together, add some samples, a little bit of Tonematrix, some Rasselbock, Tube and Crusher and it ends on … PIZZA
Oh my gosh ... there is a lot. I listen to the radio and watch TV.
Next year (hopefully)
In 2007 I was looking for some Flash web sites and found Andre Michelle's lab with the Tonematrix. I made some Tonematrix live session tracks and I fell in love with audiotool. That's it.

Audiotool is a gift, a wonderful parcel. Free, without adverts, no time-limit, comprehensive, accessible for everyone. The fact that Audiotool works with this amount of data is initself a miracle for me. Ok, I can't upload complete audio tracks, of course the libary of other DAWs is larger and possibly, but only possibly, certain functions more elaborate. I like the tools, the modular system with the synth on whitenoise and more than anything the cabling and usability. And then we can publish our works in the community. And last but not least we can invite people to collaborate on a track, and learn from others. Where else is that possible?

The bit of spam is negligible. If it gets too much I can mute the troublemaker. But I only used this facility once: the community is just great.

If help is needed, you get it immediately. Or you can look at the video tutorials on Youtube.

For this and much more to the Aduiotool crew and the community a big

THANK YOU!

interview.a: I like to cover any genre that passes up in front of me and one thing that's apparent is that I try to make danceable/close-to-mainstream tracks. My style was somehow called "Bluelectro" by some listeners. Also I take from Reversal's style of adding exciters to almost everything. Savant is one of my main outside inspirations because of the 8-bit melodies he makes. Also, anime OST's inspire me as well. In short, fast saw-wave melody + gritty bass + 4x4 drums + Blue = my style.

I learned drums when I lived in Indonesia in 2004-2006 (so when I was about 7 years old), but I forgot how to play them now. I learned piano in 2006, the year I immigrated to the USA. Since then, I listened to classical music until I had a piano teacher that taught me how to play piano for a year. I still play piano but I can't read notes anymore, so I learn and play by ear. I had no experience nor knew anything about electronically made music until I discovered Audiotool.

Fall of 2011....or 2012. I don't remember very well but I know it was a few weeks after my birthday and around the same time Uprising joined. By the end of 2010 I started listening to electronic music. Soon I wanted to make my own music and an anonymous user from some random website that I can't remember suggested Audiotool. Once I checked it out, there was no way I wouldn't make music. "Bluedude" was a joke name. I just chose it because I liked the color blue and I am a dude. Now the Audiotool people remember me by it. Bahahaha!

Audiotool is such a blessing. It's probably (if not) the BEST free DAW (Digital Audio Workstation) out there. Actually, it is the best DAW in my opinion because of the amount of great tracks able to be produced and the amazing community. Seriously. The community is a family...albeit a strangely lovable family. To my friends who heard some of my tracks, thanks for listening. For the growing Audiotoolers, you will get a lot better but it takes so much practice and patience. I'm sure you already know what I mean or you will know soon. Thanks Audiotool for featuring me as an artist! I never would have thought that I would get featured because a lot of people said that I would, but it never came. I learned that making music isn't about attention, it's about what YOU want to make when YOU want it. It's about passing time doing something you love. It's about bacon...ear bacon (because ear candy is too mainstream). Audiotool should be a hobby, not a job. Also, stay happy and stay fresh! Peace!

I never tend to stick to one genre, however. "Relaxing" is what I'd call my style.

I spent, like, weeks trying to find some kind of cool but free DAW that I can easily make music with. One day I went on the Google shop thingy, and I found Audiotool on january 16th, 2012.
Don't worry about what other people think. Make what you wanna make... Oh, and I love all of you guys, as people.

intro: The dark figure of Agrippa stands out a mile in our colourful mixture. Reminiscent of the Gothic Music of the late 1980s, his New Wave Sound is chock full of the spirit of that decade and would not be out of place in any contemporary record collection. His eminently experienced Synth work is rounded off by his own vocals, oozing with a goodly portion of Weltschmerz. A real must, and that not just for listeners in dark clothes.
about:
Richard
Place: Originally, from Liverpool, but for some time now I've lived just outside London, and I work in London.
Age: 42. It is the answer to the ultimate question of life, the universe and everything.
Profession: I studied art and design, but now I work at an advertising agency, heading the creative team, using my powers for evil.

interview.a:
I suppose it could be categorised as minimal synth, synth wave, DIY, new wave, vintage synth pop. Occasionally I touch on EDM, but more often I start off with melodies and vocal ideas now, rather than beats and basslines.

I'm really into vintage, analogue sounds. Software synths can often do a great interpretation of analogue sounds – the Pulverisateur being a fantastic example – I love that instrument.

However, over the past year, I've also started purchasing a few physical analogue instruments. I love them. I think a lot of it is down to the small inaccuracies, especially in pitch. And I love the feeling of sounds directly created by turning dials and moving sliders, manipulating voltages.

So, increasingly, physical devices feature in a lot of my tracks on Audiotool, especially over the past 9 months. But I keep coming back to the Audiotool staples, especially the Pulverisateur, which often remains an instrument of choice.

I suppose my period in the 90s as a post-punk and Goth obsessive has stayed with me to a degree. In the 00s I was more into Electro and House. However, more than anything, I think the earlier music of my childhood had a profound and lasting impact on me. OMD, Tubeway Army, The Human League and many others. I vividly remember first hearing their early records (and radio play) as a child in Liverpool – around the 1978-1982 period.

I had a college band in the early to mid 90s, very guitar driven and post-punk and new wave in style. Though electronic was part of it – I used a drum machine and lots of "synth" – actually just a cheap, Yamaha presets keyboard, fed through a chorus pedal and a reverb. Back then creating electronic music just wasn't anywhere near as accessible (or affordable) as it is now.

I largely stopped by the end of the 90s. Probably because my attention shifted from music and nights out towards building a career in design.

At the start of 2013 two things happened. First, I met a few musicians/performers, initially online, then later in person, who were creating fantastic electronic, avant-garde music and I started to feel inspired to create music again.

But the real spark was discovering Audiotool at the same time. I was at a sister-in-law's house, feeling extremely bored, so I was browsing the Android app store and I came across the Audiotool Sketch app. I loved it, and then very soon discovered Audiotool itself.

My very first track on Audiotool was called "Sieben". I shared it with one of those musicians, who was very encouraging, and it was even featured on a small compilation CD, that was handed out as part of an exhibition on DIY music and new independent labels.

A few months later, I had an instrumental album released, physical and digital, on a little indie label.

I also collaborated on a cover version of 'Nowhere to Run' with Ivan Antunovic. I supplied the backing track, entirely produced on Audiotool, and he provided the vocals. It remains part of his set.

In 2014 I was invited to perform at Fluc in Vienna and Abattoir in London. It was never my intention to do gigs (or to sing), but as I was asked, I thought I'd give it a go. I took a physical synth, but in large part I was singing to digital backing tracks, produced using Audiotool. I really enjoyed it – a real buzz. I don't plan to start touring ort anything, but a few gigs a year would be fun.

interview.a: My electronic style of music is geared toward Ambient/Atmospheric/Lo-Fi sound mixed with a lot of other stuff... just basically anything I make I always try to take the listener to another place.
I've been playing piano since age 6
Started scribbling notes around 6 as well but didn't start writing actual music till about a year or so after that.
My brother saw Audiotool on the Chrome app store or something like 3 years ago and thought it looked cool, I looked at it and I thought it was pretty cool.

I think Audiotool is the perfect place to have fun and let your creativity run wild, and I've found there are so many things you can do with electronic music that you can't with the classical approach to writing music for instruments and stuff. When you're writing electronic music I would advise you to listen to lots of different electronic artists and also listen to classical music. Listening to electronic music really helps me find inspiration for different sounds I could make, different textures and stuff, and the classical music helps me with the actual compositional technique; I try to get into the composers head and wonder what they were thinking, why they are doing what they are doing with the music. Just listen and learn from what is out there, keep doing it until you get better and above all have fun. :)
