tracks.desc: I really wish enjoyyourshoes would keep making stuff like this, very beautiful and unique.

tracks.name: dapper
tracks.desc: I couldn't find my favorite track by him cuz he has like 500 so I just put this. It's still perfect, it's cool how he’s able to make so much awesome music while still using so much repetition.

tracks.name: Northern comfort
tracks.desc: This one is just awesome, it's repetitive but keeps the listener engaged. And I especially love the sound design and the detail of it. It's really unique and sorta lo-fi. and the movement of it all is great too.

tracks.name: We Fall
tracks.desc: Very beautiful track and awesome use of vocal samples.

tracks.name: Tides
tracks.desc: This track I really love. Just amazing.

tracks.name: ノスタルジア
tracks.desc: I love this, it's so tranquil but what's cool is that the sample is actually Clair de Lune and he was able to transform it into something completely different and equally beautiful.

tracks.name: high places
tracks.desc: At first I didn't like this, sounded too pop-like/EDM-ish but actually it's grown on me. The percussion could be fixed a bit but other than that I like it. It's pretty accessible and sounds pretty good too.

intro:
It's about four years ago that a kid calling himself 'The Crazy Druminator' made his first tentative steps on our app. One name-change and 138 tracks (resulting in 11205 likes) later, Astrum has reached the peak of Audiotool popularity and long since stretched out his feelers towards the big stage.
At the audiotool.con we had the opportunity to experience the energy of his music in a breathtaking live performance which convinced us all: this guy is going places
about:
Jackson Hale
Place: Albuquerque, New Mexico
Age: I am 20 years old at the moment
University: I am studying Recording Arts at the University of Colorado in Denver

interview.a: My style is all over the place, never sticking to a distinct genre. Nowadays it lies somewhere between EDM and IDM and takes on whatever form my mood currently is.

I started playing piano when I was 5 years old, and moved onto drums when I was 9 or so. Since then I've lost nearly all of my piano skills. I played drums in a lot of jazz bands when I was in high school and put in a good deal of time in symphonic type bands as well as an instrumental metal band my friends and I had.

I began making music electronically when I was 15. I stumbled upon Andre Michelle's website which showed me the (in)famous Tonematrix. From there, I followed a link to the Hobnox site, found Audiotool, and was instantly hooked on the concept of electronic music production.
Do, or at least try, anything and everything you can think to do. The more absurd, the better.

tracks.name: Airglow
tracks.desc: Kepz is able to achieve a depth with his music that most aren't capable of. This holds true with Airglow, a dynamically lush and airy journey from beginning to end that takes you out of the chair in which you're probably sitting. I hope to meet this guy some day.

tracks.name: Family
tracks.desc: This is about as good as it gets when it comes to collaborations on Audiotool. Jace (J. Fields) supplies a flowering backdrop of pads and light percussion samples while Julsy saturates the scene with her gorgeous drifting voice and thoughtful lyrics.

tracks.name: Line ∞
tracks.desc: Perspective displays his wonderful composition abilities in this one. The two-step styled drums fit perfectly with the melody and crushed bass elements that weave in and out of the patterns. This one's gentle, but it's bound to get stuck in your head.

tracks.name: Spite
tracks.desc: The feeling this song invokes is so unique; a blend of nostalgia and anguish. It also makes you question the timing and tempo as it switches through drum grooves of differing paces. The last resolve when the original beat kicks back in with an additional lower harmony hits home every time.

tracks.name: AFTERGLOW ((Jonjon Mix) + deep1tone)
tracks.desc: While we're on the subject of uplifting Drum and Bass, this track will really have you flying. 3 great minds put energy into this thing and its clear. The whole Afterglow remix contest brought about some pretty awesome creations.

tracks.name: teeth.
tracks.desc: I had to have rnzr on this list. This guy... not only is he great fun to be around, he has created some of the most monstrous combinations of tools and cables the audiotool app has ever seen, with a sound to match. This track was absolutely jaw dropping when I first heard it, and still is. rnzr. creates music with an extremely high level of mindfulness and creativity.

tracks.name: Cheerful tunes for the end of summer
tracks.desc: There are so many friends and artists that I love from Audiotool that deserve a spot on this list. As I was browsing my

intro: The Seahorse's first publication about a year ago, was more like a Machinist-based finger exercises than a complete track. But it was cleary the work of an experienced rhythm man. In this short space of time he has managed to enhance his skills so much that he can now create music which any movie director would give his eye teeth for.
about:
Mark Wells
Place: Denver, Colorado, USA
Age: Almost six (in dog years)
Profession: Recent Environmental Science grad seeking bartending position

interview.a: I don't really know how to describe my style or what genre it is. Sort of a mix between Ambient, Techno, Psychedelic, Acid, World, Big Beat, and an assortment of different live music. Is that a genre? Probably. ;)
I've spent most of my life playing drums in everything from marching band to Punk and Metal Bands with a little bit of Jazz, Funk, and Latin thrown in here or there.
In the 3rd grade with the saxophone and quickly decided that I wanted to play drums instead. My parents were thrilled with that decision.

I discovered Audiotool through the Chrome store. I downloaded the app and promptly forgot about it. I rediscovered it a few months later and was immediately hooked! The Machiniste was the coolest thing I had ever seen ... Then I discovered The Pulv!!!! I had absolutely no idea how to use it and no idea how subtractive synthesis worked, so I became obsessed and read everything I could about the subject and understood nothing. That led to tons of experimentation and relentless studying of other AT artists tracks and I finally began to learn! Yeehaw!!

This thing we call Audiotool is amazing! Not only is it a powerful DAW, It comes with an international community of open minded and generous folks who are more than willing to let you look at how they make their music, and give you advice and feedback. It also comes with people who will listen to your tracks and an almost unlimited catalog of tunes for your own enjoyment. Besides, how many of your friends can tell you they just finished writing a song with a dude from Macedonia. :)

tracks.name: The Hope
tracks.desc: When I think of White Grizzly I picture a man living deep in a eternally snow filled mountain range, a man who snowshoes forty miles uphill just to find an internet signal with which to publish his apocalyptically beautiful soundscapes. I was going to pick a different track by him initially, but then he published this one the other day and it took the honors!

tracks.name: Low Pressure
tracks.desc: Syntax was one of the first artists I got into on AT. The way she writes her music just really makes a lot of sense to me. I basically learned how to use the pulv by opening her tracks and studying them, not to mention sidechaining, velocity and a bunch of other stuff I use on a regular basis. Low pressure is still one of my favorites by her. I love everything about it. The bass, the percs, and that clean clean clean Syntax sound!

tracks.name: Untitled
tracks.desc: Mikke is a genius. His sound design is some of the best and most inspiring I've heard on AT. Not to mention, he's an incredibly nice and humble guy. This track says more than I ever could about the brilliance of his music.

tracks.name: ground control
tracks.desc: Speaking of incredibly nice guys.... Tornsage: Broken Philosopher, selfless promoter of other artists, and eternal supporter of new ATers. One never knows exactly what to expect from a Tornsage track, but you always know it's going to be GREAT!!! Thanks for all of the support, Torn! :)

intro: For about three years now MCT has worked his way through a range of widely differing Electro styles. From House via Trance to Ambient and - inspired by his great Audiotool idol VLTRA - even New Garage. His repertoire expands from track to track. If you remind yourself that he got to know Audiotool as a musical greenhorn, his progress has been considerable so that he has made his way into the community's heart.
about:
Tenji Tembo
Place: Baltimore, Maryland, USA
Age: 21
Profession: Student/ UX Developer @ Must Win LLC

interview.a: My style can best be described as a story: When I first started fiddling with Audiotool, I was heavy into the dubstep scene, so I focused on creating intense tracks with crazy bass-lines, under the name DJ-240. Then suddenly, everyone I knew was doing dubstep, so I sought to re-invent myself. I went from DJ-240 to MCTembo, and with that, a change of style. MCTembo focused on Ambient and Future Garage (influenced by artists such as VLTRA, Olafd, and enjoyyourshoes), which lead to the release of Simplicity EP. From there MCTembo ventured deeper into pure ambience, with the release of Isolation and Depths EP. After my foray with Ambience and Future Garage, I began to experiment again. It was around the time trap was up and coming, as well as my discovery of Deep House (thanks to Cihangir, and Mikel Boyles aka Nowaii) ending with the release of Future House EP as MCT. Who knows what the future holds?
I have a fairly light musical background, stemming mainly from piano lessons as a young child. I did about 3-4 years of lessons, as well as a year experimenting with other various instruments.

I really got into making music when I discovered Audiotool, as they provided the platform and the tools to make whatever ran through my imagination, and translate my inspirations into melodies others can enjoy.
Remember Burn Studios? I discovered Audiotool during their Sasha Remix Competition. #Throwback. I wonder what happened to them lol

You guys are amazing. I've never seen such a vibrant and diverse community such as Audiotool. The ability to make, share, learn, and grow as musicians via the internet is a blessing to be had. The feeling of elation you get when you discover the next best thing (like that time I found Intracktion and Claine) and want to shout it out to the rest of the world, it's moments like those that make me excited to use the site. Keep making music. Keep inspiring me. And maybe, just maybe, I'll do one more EP :p

tracks.name: Allure
tracks.desc: The definition of a lazy summer afternoon. The perfect kickback song when it’s 3pm on a Sunday. Just you, the homies, some beer, and the sun shining down. That is Allure.

tracks.name: ｗｈｙ ｄｉｄ ｔｈｅ ｌｉｇｈｔ ｆａｄｅ
tracks.desc: Raw Emotion. Raw, Schizophrenic Emotion. It takes a mad scientist, to construct the story this song tells. Ignore the technical marvels, and lose yourself to the emotion this song engulfs you with.

tracks.name: the last thing
tracks.desc: Witch House is a unique thing, and there are very few that can understand and showcase it’s brilliance. Nominal is Witch House in my opinion. The Last Thing sets an amazing tone and ambience that can’t be replicated by any other artist. The infusion of different styles really sell this as one of his best tracks.

tracks.name: [Phresh.]
tracks.desc: I’m fairly certain OBN is signed. This track is one of the many reasons why he is signed. That theme song you have when you walk down the street, and you know everybody gives a crap about you? This is it. On repeat for the 344th time.

Experimentation. Experimentation drives innovation. Devoid ¾ was my first touch into Future Garage, and my first exposure to the legend we all know as VLTRA. Plus, Natalie Portman :p and the original is just as epic.

intro: Together with Frigolito, Abstract is probably the user with most Swing characteristics on Audiotool. Expertly he mixes basslines reminiscent of Jazz with elements of Dub-Step, Electro House and Drum and Bass, and that with great success: with almost 500 Favourites, his track 'Illuminati Lumberjacks' is one of the all-time chart-breakers of our website. A must on any playlist
about:
Nathan Glaser
Place: Tulsa, Oklahoma (USA)
Age: 16
School: Booker T. Washington High School

interview.a: Well, since my musical development has primarily happened through jazz, almost all tracks that I write (and my style in general) is heavily jazz-influenced. As an EDM producer, I was first majorly inspired by Savant, and as result a lot of my tracks feature 8 bit type solos and such. Mord Fustang inspired me to write big electro chord-based songs. Once I discovered glitch hop, it fit perfectly with my jazzy style, and I like to write a lot of glitch hop. Overall though, I try to become good at as many genres as possible so that I can be a versatile artist, and now I write songs depending on what I'm liking at that moment.

Both my parents are professional musicians, and I grew up singing in various choirs, as well as playing piano for a year when I was fairly young (somewhere from 6-8 years old), which I then hated but now admittedly wished I had followed through on. From 6th grade to present time I've played trumpet at school, and from 9th to present I've played in my high school's Jazz band.

Sometime from January to March in 2012, one of my friends found out about Audiotool and decided that we should try messing around on it. We both started out as total production noobs and learned from each other for a little bit. I started looking around the site and naturally found Infyuthsion (since he is and probably always will be the most popular artist of Audiotool), as well as Pilgrim. I got super inspired by, well, simply how awesome their music was. Ever since then, I've been having fun on Audiotool and trying to constantly get better.

There's always going to be someone out there that makes amazing music that you can be inspired by. Inspiration is key in getting ideas to flow. Learning from each other is very important to the continuing development of practically anyone as a musical artist :)

tracks.name: tranquility
tracks.desc: Don't let the title fool you; this is one of the most hardcore and eclectic neuro-hop tracks I've ever heard. The first drop is reminiscent of Billain's style, while the second drop is head-bobbingly funky with extremely well-honed syncopation. Luxior did an outstanding job here, and think the joke element to the track is great as well :P

tracks.name: Nerd
tracks.desc: A nice little fun jazzy dnb piece reminds me of a spy chase scene of some kind. However, the production quality is sublime, with unique and sophisticated chords and synthwork. Hats off to Gno4166.

tracks.name: wait
tracks.desc: This is a track that invokes pure and raw emotion. The pad and bass combination is really just outstanding. The pitched vocals in the background make for some great subtle touches and help complete the bittersweet and sad vibe. Overall a prime example of the amazing future garage that Audiotool has to offer.

tracks.name: vitamine DnB
tracks.desc: THRONE (or PLUS+, the name that I originally knew him by) has crafted some of the most professional-sounding liquid dnb on Audiotool, this track included. The moving bassline is so creative, and the typical PLUS+ plucks are meaty as ever. Oh, and don't forget that amazingly atmospheric vibe in the intro and the airy leads. And that dang swing halftime. The fact that this track was fairly overlooked compared to other amazing liquid tracks by him makes no sense to me.

tracks.name: Firefly (Ft. Luxior)
tracks.desc: Yo, so I liked this chord progression so much that I learned to play it on piano. The suspension at the end of the progression is just so dang tasteful. Oh yeah, and the chord chops are absolutely nuts. Overall, an amazing fast paced banger that has been on replay for me since 7 months ago.

intro: SouthBronxDen has been an Audiotool elemental force since its earliest days. His consistently individualistic version of Funk and Jazz in all its scope is his main distinguishing feature and over the years has become his very own style. He skillfully manoeuvers around musical norms and comes up with a genre-mix which can perhaps best be described as Den-Fusion.
about:
Dennis Davis
Place: South Bronx, New York (USA)
Age: I am to old to be alive (62) but I still love to party
Profession: My profession I really don't have one but I do a lot of different things, like working for a hospice, for an airline company, for a shipping company et al. I have sold some paintings I have done. I guess the place I learned and gained the most knowledge from is Audiotool.

interview.a: My musical style is anything goes from classical to Funk, Funk is my fave.
My musical background is listening to all types of music and loving them.

I started making music on Hobnox and followed Audiotool to it's new platform.

I purchased some Fake Books and some on theory and started to use them in my attempts to make music. I am still learning.
Like I mentioned before I started with Audiotool from the Hobnox days when an e-friend ask me to check out his tracks, I have been hooked ever since.

tracks.name: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe
tracks.desc: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe by enjoyyourshoes is a very successful experimental track

tracks.name: Papa is an Audiotooler
tracks.desc: Papa is an Audiotooler (remix) by Fontana showcases why he is known as the

tracks.name: escape
tracks.desc: Escape by Invisible Green Spider aka DroneAlpha combines thought provoking music and takes the listener to a place of his soul he didn't know he had

tracks.name: Summit
tracks.desc: Summit by Mad-Scientist-Mike takes you to the top, and the bass work keeps your feet rooted firmly to the dance floor

tracks.name: Can't Get Enough of Your Love
Alive Comeback
tracks.desc: I really don't have a favorite track of my own but I remember a track I did back when Hobnox was around called

tracks.name: In A Trance(the day my son was born)

intro: To call Tornsage an Audiotool legend is really not exaggerating: every one of his tracks raises the standard in the Ambient field a notch higher, showing us again and again how much magically destroyed atmosphere can be conjured out of old familiar piano samples. Beside his enormous talent he is remarkable for his friendly presence in the community: he gives new arrivals a warm welcome and makes them feel at home in Audiotool. Check out the man with the rabbit head!
about:
John Ertsgard
Place: I was born in SanJose California but moved east as a teen to Massachusetts where I have lived all my life since.
Age: I have been around the sun 55 times but feel like I just got started.
Profession: I am an Electronics Engineer and currently work at Bose Corporation in the division creating automotive sound system designs.

interview.a: After I finished my stint in the USMC, I started a band with some fellow musicians named "Johnny Nervous and the Shapeless Pygmies" (rhythm guitar/vocals) playing covers and originals in local clubs. Following that I jammed extensively with my son Nick (drummer, keyboardist) and now I work on my piano and audiotool exclusively.
I started playing guitar as a kid and sang songs like "Yellow Submarine" in a 5th grade band early on.

At the time I was jamming regularly with my son he turned me on to the audiotool DAW. After some time and a couple first tries I was absolutely hooked and have not looked back since. The site replaced oodles of equipment that I had lost, given away or thrown out over the years thus presenting an amazing opportunity to rejoin an earlier effort to create music without having to compromise.

My message is thank you. Thank you all for being so kind, helpful and inspiring over the years. Thank you for the vast array of music and for opening my eyes to new genres that I had never considered. The community at Audiotool is diverse and talented and I will always be grateful to have such an amazing place to work on music and share ideas with others as well as a gifted group of SW professionals who designed and run the site. I truly think that Audiotool is set for greatness and will heartily compete with alternate DAWs in the future.

tracks.name: When Stars Collide
tracks.desc: RNZR’s best in my opinion

tracks.name: Shut up and Fuck
tracks.desc: Elia Trix – a masterful producer who I wish would come back and continue.

tracks.name: The Snow
tracks.desc: I had to include the beautiful work of vile – always an amazing mix and a great influence.

tracks.name: please
tracks.desc: Amoeba – what more can one say than what has already been said – a true talent and a beautiful piece of music.

tracks.name: The Hunt Is On ( Hunter X Hunter Bump )
tracks.desc: I included this because I love everything offbeat does – this was an especially influential bump probably because it was some of the first work I heard from him. One of the best artists on audiotool.

tracks.name: The sound of the time
tracks.desc: The grizz in a deeply enigmatic artist who’s music has no parallel and who has influenced me with everything he produces.

tracks.name: Monoscope
tracks.desc: This is a track I collaborated with invisible green spider on and to this day remains one of my favs – I think it has a lot to do with the story or the idea behind the track.

tracks.name: ravenshead
tracks.desc: > <script type=

intro: Interview unfortunately not available.

tracks.name: bobby
tracks.desc: I have been hooked to this track since I listened to it for the first time. This one is like reading a book, it tells a story. And has some of the best bassline, leads, pads and percussions I've ever heard.

tracks.name: KTN-34
tracks.desc: I'd have liked to include all of KaTaNa production in this edition, but I'm only limited to ten :) His tracks are just technically perfect and his sounds breathtaking. I learned so much by just listening to his tracks, let alone opening them! He helped me a lot in understanding why and how to use filters, EQs, etc. Thanks man!

tracks.name: techno breakfast
tracks.desc: Christian-Chrom is the bomb! You can feel the energy punching you in the face while his tracks are playing. Combine this with great technical skills and productivity (he got currently 82 tracks in less than a year!) and you got the basic Christian-Chrom.

tracks.name: Synthetik
tracks.desc: in5omniac is another AT Techno hero. His clean and powerful sounds build up a gritty minimal Techno whose rhythm will shake your bones. This one is just a great example of how he's going to become a famous producer ;)

tracks.name: Vellicator
tracks.desc: I love how Alan Ross took some noises and arranged them in such a beauty! I think this track was made to introduce the Rasselbock if I remember correctly.

intro: Navor is one of a group of highly talented young American EDM producers on Audiotool and initiator of the very successful and heartwarming mega collaboration for a sick member of the community. In the wide range of Electronic Dance Music he doesn't want to nail himself down to one particular style. So better expect the unexpected.
about:
Orion Hendershot
Place: Currently living in Ft. Myers, Florida
Age: 16
School: Sophomore at South Fort Myers High School

interview.a: Honestly, I don't even know myself. I go from Dubstep to Ambient to Trap to Future Bass, I'm really kind of all over the place. I just enjoy producing everything trying to actually define a style for myself, so I go all over genres trying to find something that I can fall in love with.

I played violin from 4th to 8th grade, but that never really went anywhere. I recently have been trying to teach myself to play piano, so there's that! Before I was listening to electronic music I was listening to Metal a lot, which was what introduced me to electronic music when one of my favorite bands mentioned Deadmau5 in an interview or something. I listened to Deadmau5 a lot when I first got into electronic music, and just branched out from there.

I started making music when I was 12, so four years is coming up within the next year. I started with Looplabs if anyone remembers that monstrosity, then I found Audiotool and let loose. I've been using other DAWs recently such as Ableton and FL Studio, as well.

I discovered Audiotool when I was 12, but not really sure when, but I think it was somewhere in the middle of summer so guessing June or something? I found Audiotool by browsing Google for "Free online music maker". I thought Audiotool sounded like a cool name so I signed up and started messing around. I've had a few accounts before my current one, but those are lost in the past (thankfully).

Just keep your head up. Music is something that should come from within, so always try your hardest to better yourself and others around you. Critique yourself and always try to expand your horizons. Audiotool has a ton of stuff to offer, and even more with the coming updates. Always interact with the community, making friends on Audiotool is one of the best things to ever happen in my life. I've made so many close friends and even met one in real life, which is incredible! The community is one of the best things that Audiotool has over other DAWs and music sharing sites, so my final advice is to take advantage of it!

tracks.name: Nor
tracks.desc: This track is craaaaaazy good. The amount of detail put into the background is astonishing. Not to mention the bass is incredibly good, one of my favorite collaborations on Audiotool.

tracks.name: The Final Scene
tracks.desc: I've loved this track ever since the day it came out. I listen to this track so much you'd think it'd be the most annoying thing on earth to me, but no. I can't ever get enough of this track, and xav never fails to impress.

tracks.name: Last Chance
tracks.desc: One of the most professionally produced songs on Audiotool. The setup, organization, sound design, chords, melodies, etc.. Everything about this just does it for me, really great.

tracks.name: Most Popular Song Of The Hour
tracks.desc: This track is sooooo goooooood. The melody during the drop gets me literally every time I listen. Crazy professional.

tracks.name: Anna (Notoz Remix)
tracks.desc: One of the most beautiful tracks I've heard on audiotool. Great spin on the original.

intro: To describe Pathfnder's style only as Retro would not do justice to his wild and always dynamic mixture. But what in fact is ever present is the character of the Roller Discos and Arcades of the late seventies and early eighties, enhanced with a trace of the Modern. And that at the highest level of production techniques. So pick up your joysticks and follow his path on the way to the Highscore.
about:
Metin Filiz
Place: St.Gallen/Swizerland
Age: 32
Profession: Dispatcher / Technical Support engineer

interview.a: I think most producers are influenced by the music they hear and grow up with. Maybe that's why my tracks are a mix of Funk and 80s Synth wave. I love this pushing feeling with strong beats and the characteristic bass and clear synth design.
I learned to play the drums and keyboard for a few years.

My first contact with something like a DAW was in 1994 with a program called Magix Music Maker. Back then it was more of a loop puzzle game with a small library. Producing my own stuff began in 2007 when I discovered Ableton and the possibility to record and work with instruments

I discovered Auditool some years ago at a time where I was bored with pre-designed synth patches but had no idea how to create my own. I thought that my mix would be this blurred because of the bad patches. It was on YouTube that I found a tutorial for the pulv - I think it was by Einhard Luther.

It is really awesome how many talented and creative artists there are in this community! Keep listening, commenting and learning from each other. This is one of the strongest parts of Audiotool I think. Publishing songs on Soundcloud is not half as satisfying as it is on Audiotool, because of the real feedback of the community. Don't underestimate Audiotool and invest a lot of money in "professional" music workstations early on. A painter does not automatically produce a "better" picture with more expensive brushes without knowledge about colors and proportions.

tracks.name: Airwaves
tracks.desc: Whoa! this track is so powerful and pushing! This is the soundtrack I would choose if I had to run from helicopters shooting with laser beams. I think my heartbeat would have had a dropout on the break at 1:55.

tracks.name: Airwaves (Teqtoniq Remix)
tracks.desc: Wow...this is just brutal! Loved the original and this gave me the rest. Love the buildup so much and then the drop with the vocals ...crazy… No, seriously this is epic, very clean synths and a great mixing quality.

tracks.name: Highway Cruisin'
tracks.desc: This track could be directly out of my Spotify track list. Melodically and funky Synth Pop with an overwhelming professionalism. I`m in love with the synth design and the clever alternations in the melody.

tracks.name: Coming Back To You
tracks.desc: Jordi Moragues ...what an impressive artist! I would have preferred to list all of his tracks here. The emotionality in his work and his talent for playing with the expectations of the audience is unique. I chose this track due to the beautiful fusion of perfect Audiotool synth work and this wonderful violin record.

tracks.name: white lies
tracks.desc: It is absolutely mind-blowing how genius Luxior works with his groove and various puzzle pieces in his tracks. Every second you hear an unexpected noise that fits perfectly into the shuffled rhythm structure. Outstanding mix and synth work combined with lots of talent and passion.

tracks.name: Ghetto Blaster
tracks.desc: Retro music at its best! Wonderful job with the synths and percussion in this track. I love this retro Synth Wave style and he captured it with perfection.

tracks.name: Bottleneck
tracks.desc: I could listen to this track for hours, it has a great groove and some of the cleanest and best-mixed synths on Audiotool. The creative note pattern never loses the attention of the listener and brings new elements throughout .

tracks.name: why did i fade away
tracks.desc: This song has everything. I love the dreamy atmosphere and the on-point percussion. The synth design and the composition blows me away every time I listen to this piece of art. Opaqity has the talent to create something that is more than the sum of its parts.

tracks.name: solace
tracks.desc: Solace by Tornsage is one of those tracks that make you forget time and the environment around you. Every note is a step more in this emotional dream world he is creating. It’s like reading a book of emotions. Sad and full of hope at the same time.

intro: Since the early days Daftwill has been a well liked member of the community and producer of suspense-packed dark Electro and Techno tracks. Among the sources of his input were a number of Audiotool greats of those days, as can be seen from the number of collaborations he has been involved in. With sixteen collaborations to date he must be the record holder on Audiotool.
about:
William Lachoua
Place: I'm from France. I'm have been staying in New Caledonia for a year but I actually live in Paris.
Age: 20
Profession: I have been working in a gas station for eight months. In October I will start studying at an audiovisual school and attempt to become a sound engineer.

interview.a: I don't really know how to define my style: I try to vary it for each new track I make, but some guys say that my tracks have some kind of dark, trippy and psych sounds.

I never played or composed music before Audiotool, but I like listening to music all day long: from Beethoven to Neu!, through Dave Brubeck, Thomas Newman, Pink bFloyd and Fluke. I like to discover some old and new stuff from any style every day.

I discovered Audiotool in a french High-Tech magazine more than 5 ago, when it was still named Hobnox-Audiotool. At that time, I didn't publish tracks. I just played around with the Tonematrix and the Bassline.

Audiotool is really a powerful website, not just because you have total access to unlimited music production tools. Only your personal creativity sets your limits. But especially because the community is really nice: attentive and helpful for helping you improve. From amateur users to master producers, there will always be people to listen and comment on your pieces.

tracks.name: trip 17

about:
Robin B.
Place: Altusried, Bavaria, Germany
Age: 20
Profession: Kempten University of Applied Sciences

interview.a:
Of course this changes depending on my mood, but it's usually fast, bassy, experimental, tense, a bit empty, sometimes uncanny. I love the Dead Space game series and sometimes I try to transport these eerie feelings in my music, usually unconsciously. Maybe you felt like there's something missing in my tracks. This is often intended. The reason is difficult to describe. I like this feeling of something strange happening. The absence of the expected consequence of something. A good example is "Silent Anomalies". In case it shows itself, it can still be strange. It's there, you can see it, but it's not normal. You don't want to touch it by any means. Like rotten food. Haha I feel insane now as I write it, but you probably know what I mean. These feelings are so interesting to me as they always make my flesh crawl. It's pretty much the opposite of a love song.

I love subtle melodies in tracks mixed with "breathed" sounds and short samples, so that's what I often want. Some tracks by other artists have short pieces repeated over and over, they can sound equally great.

I tried to play contrabass once but stopped. I never wanted to commit myself to one specific instrument. Sometimes I grab my keyboard and try to play songs I remember from movies or games, or just for fun. I can't play chords and melody at the same time, though. Middle eastern and Chinese instruments are very interesting to me as they look and sound different to what I'm used to. I'm really fascinated by the idea of sounds one never heard before, not melodies. Yes, melodies help, but for me these are "just" 12 different notes in different octaves in different orders, with little freedom. You'll probably notice that when listening to my tracks. Some of them need more melodic elements. If you listen to tracks like "Non Human" by Phace & Misanthrop you'll notice that there is a subtle melody in it. That's one of my goals.
I started with a demo of the "MAGIX Music Maker" at my aunt's computer. They all went to a party and I had to stay home. It wasn't that bad. I was 11 or 12 years old.

Yes, Google. I can't remember the search string exactly but it was something like "make music online". I knew it theoretically was possible. Audiotool says it was 4 years ago, but for me it was at least 5. I did not expect a whole working studio :)

Don't be afraid of messing your track up while editing it. I am afraid and it does not help. It's probably better to just export parts as samples and use these. You'll treat them differently. You won't see them as a huge and complicated network of devices anymore, but a simple sample. I don't do that so you can see how I did it. I don't like the idea of a huge flat sample base either. You can go even further and work in iterations, but this is experimental and not tested yet. I'll try it out.
Thank you for this amazing and completely free application and website. There is no better way to show people what is possible with music and the web and what it's about.

real_name: Joa D. Ebert. The D is for Diego
intro: Joa Ebert is not only a very talented programmer and musician, he is also Audiotool developer of the first hour and father of the first track released on Audiotool. We still consider Joa as a part of the Audiotool team even if he has now already founded his own company with Tim Richter. Defrac is the foundation for the HTML 5 version of Audiotool to be released later this year. Thanks for all your hard work. Without you, Audiotool would never had been possible.
about:
Joa D. Ebert
Place: Originally from Bielefeld, Germany. You could say it is the modern Atlantis, the town that is nowhere to be found. But nowadays I am living in Cologne, Germany
Age: 28

interview.q: What is your job at Audiotool?
interview.a: I founded Audiotool and worked on it for about five years but our ways parted – no bridges burned though. My main job at Audiotool was the core foundation of the application. I worked mostly on algorithms and data structures. But I did basically everything from drawing little monsters, building and shipping the browser app to developing the website and backend. I also wrote some of the "cool" parts – the desktop and cable rendering for instance. I also developed my personal favorite: the Rasselbock. It went through many iterations: from a first completely autonomous cellular automata, playing stutter effects to what we have now (André also applied his Midas touch to the DSP implementation). I left Audiotool to start a new company called defrac. My co-founder Tim Richter and I are developing a platform to convert compiled Java code to different target platforms like the web, iOS or Android. This is quite technical but really fun stuff! I heard through the grapevine that Audiotool Next is built with defrac

interview.q: How would you define your work?
interview.a: As a developer you're in the fantastic position of imagining something up and then being able to create it immediately. It is like Lego on steroids. There are no limits and once you hit a wall you have to be creative to find a solution. It's basically never boring and creating a massive Flash application like Audiotool was a real challenge. If you like solving puzzles, you can probably become a really good developer.

interview.q: What is your educational background for the job you are doing?
interview.a: Blood and sweat. I was a small child when I started programming and went the path of the autodidact. I started to study cognitive computer science once but dropped out which I kind of regret. When I find the time, I seriously want to get that computer science degree.

interview.q: When and how did you start programming?
interview.a: I think I was about eight years old. Our first computer had no case and the only way to reset the machine was by putting a screwdriver on the jump. We also had two games. GORILLA.BAS and NIBBLES.BAS. Those were written by Microsoft to demo QBasic at that time. The only way to start the game was by starting QBasic first. You always saw the source-code. The love of a lifetime emerged.

interview.q: How would you define your musical style?
interview.a: Short-circuits, buzzes and noises. Honestly I feel a dwarfed by all the talent on Audiotool and I wish I had the skills to produce the music I have in my head. I really enjoy pads and melodic synthesizers with a hint of glitch. The music I create is the music I want to listen to myself. When there are 100 listens on one of my tracks, you can be sure that I count for 96 of them.

My mother did a real good job and I am really thankful for her efforts. I went to early music education in elementary school and started playing classical piano around the age of ten. Puberty made me ditch the classical education and I went to a different music school with a focus on improvisation. I also started playing guitar there and I have a bass lying around somewhere. We did a lot of great live gigs with that school and it influenced me a lot. One person suggested a song, then someone who played the drums joined, someone who sings and so on until you have a band for that song. You meet for one or two evenings and then you play on stage. It really showed me that random people can get together and make some music. Hello live collaboration!

interview.q: When did you start making music?
interview.a: I think in 1996 or 1997 my brother and I received a fantastic christmas present. It was a copy of ReBirth RB-338. Later I started using FruityLoops and got into chiptunes for some reason.

Audiotool has the best community ever. Stay exactly like you are. Creating music makes you vulnerable. You share your songs and emotions with complete strangers. I believe that Audiotool is one of the few places on the internet where people aren't talking you down because your technique isn't perfect. Ergo the only thing I can say is that you are simply amazing and that I am proud to be one of the people who made all this possible.

PS.: Some people continue to ask why my profile is so weird. I guess one late evening there was so much work that my head was exploding and I kind of expressed it by turning my profile upside-down. When Martin joined Audiotool and took over he kindly kept this tradition!

about:
Manuel Francisco de Casas Sánchez
Place: Melilla (Spain)
Age: 18
Profession: I'm studying at university to become a teacher in the future. And if possible for sport. I really like kids and I like to teach them. Exactly, I'm studying school teaching and sports.

interview.a: As I said, I prefer to focus my music on my experiences or how I feel at that moment. So the style which I feel more connected with is Ambient/Chill. I also like Jazz, Hip Hop and Trap but my style is Ambient with soundscapes. Although if you follow my music you will see an influence of the poetry that I try to combine the music with. I really like to write with an influence from my life.

I don't have any musical background but I'm really focused on listening to music all day long. Anyway my father loves Jazz music most, so when I was a child I always listened a lot of musicians from Blues to Jazz and Rock and things like that. But I don't know anything about composition or pianos I just like to hear music.

I think that my musical development started a few months ago because I didn't have a ''style'' and I was very bad when I began but I started with my first tracks on AT like 2 years ago… actually I'm learning a lot.

It's a long story. Well one of my best friends ''Mr. Delfe'' told me that he was making music on a website, so I decided to take a look at it When he showed me his first tracks I really liked it so I started making some fun songs there.

You always need to have an inspiration, guys. If you don't have one it is better just not to waste your time. For me the most important thing is to live life to make music. You guys have to live and try to express how you can feel. Never be scared of that.

intro: Talking about Audiotool chilled Hip Hop Beats Sweattz is one of the artists who immediately come to mind. Whereby he does not restrict himself to the common practice of stringing together drum- and music loops. He skillfully combines his tracks with original syntheszier sounds and -lines giving his music an organic substance.
about:
Lance Alexander Sweatt
Place: Greenville, South Carolina
Age: 20
Profession: Majoring in Electrical Engineering at Clemson University. About to study abroad at Tohoku University in Sendai, Japan for a year.

interview.a: Sample based, Hyphy Hip Hop

My father and uncle were arguably the best rhythm section in the state, and so they made sure that from a very young age I was well versed in American Classic and Progressive Rock artists. However, once I discovered anime and video games, my interests started shifting towards more video game soundtrack music, more Japanese Pop, and less Rock & Roll. Even today, my biggest Hip Hop influences are from the Silent Hill and Samurai Champloo soundtracks. Interestingly enough, I almost exclusively listen to super poppy electro house from Japan nowadays, especially in the middle of making a chilled out Hip Hop beat on Audiotool.

My uncle gave me my first bass guitar for my 16th birthday, and then I started a band with two of my close friends. All we had were drums, bass, and keyboards, but we managed to make some interesting progressive jams.

In between band meetings, Jeff (the guy on keyboards) would show me instrumental hip hop beats from the Samurai Champloo soundtrack, as well as artists related to it. That music forever changed me, and from there on I had a deep-rooted interest in chilled out instrumental Hip Hop. Pretty soon after that, Jeff and I were sending each other beats that we found on YouTube. That was when we discovered [offbeatninja123], and through him, Audiotool.

I want to say thank you to everyone who is putting their hearts into making music here on Audiotool. Let's hope that in 10 years the community will be much bigger, and that there will be famous producers who can say they started in Audiotool!

intro: Tottenhauser is one of the Drum and Bass bedrocks of Audiotool's first hour with a huge output of dark, minimal and always driving tracks. His enthusiasm has always been infectious but focused and effective: like his initiating the first international AT get-together, 2012 in Gent. People like him are a great asset to the community being a motor for its progress and inspiration - apart from creating awesome music!
about:
Thomas Valcke
Place: Belgium
Age: 35
Profession: Product Designer

interview.a: Dark & loud atmospheric music with an emphasis on drums
None
Am I making music?

A few years ago. Back in the days when I was 18 one of my mates discovered a Rebirth demo. It was a warm welcoming from playing GTA1 all day. The demo had a time limit so in 20min you had to make all your drums and melodies and then find enough time to make a very rudimentary track. We taped all these little experiments. And listened to it until the tape was beyond salvation. From thereon I realized that everyone could make music without having to buy expensive equipment. (Instead you had to buy expensive software). So because there where better things to spend my money on, it never really got serious.

Many years, already a workingman, and probably a bit frustrated in life and work. I was in dire need of a creative outlet and thought back to those moments we had with the Rebirth demo. So I decided to go and look on the Internet for free music software. And then I stumbled upon Audiotool. I couldn't believe my eyes. It had all the features Rebirth had and a lot more! It ran in your browser and it was free! I was instantly hooked. And the first 2-years the addiction went quite far. Just ask my wife.

Nowadays I can control the addiction much better. But from time to time the urgency comes and then I know exactly where my medicine is.

Have fun, respect the community, be eternally grateful for the makers? Don't make music to be popular or rich. Don't follow trends. Don't care about followers, listens or favourites. Just be you and make your music because you need to and enjoy doing it.

tracks.name: Impact
tracks.desc: I will always know him as plus + . No matter his name this is another liquid Drum and Bass producer I’ll gladly include in my list. This track was another eye-opener for me. The first time I heard a Reese-like sub in an Audiotool track. I studied the crap out of it. Yet could never really copy it  another amazing talent.

tracks.name: ...and then all this crazy-ass robots appeared

tracks.desc: Surely Oedipax had to be on the list. She always makes the darkest Drum and Bass. This one in particular has the typical Oedipax signature. The layered drums. tracks.desc: The Amen breaks, the synths, the pacing … the energy of this track ... every time I listen to it I get goose bumps. This really was another one of those tracks that blew me away. Probably not the best-known track from Notoz but to me it is his best by far. Probably because in all the uplifting, glitched Amen drums there’s this growling dark bass telling me to run for my life ;)

tracks.desc: The guy is crazy we all know that. What better track to showcase it with. Jambam has a great talent for combining sounds and find melodies in the chaos he creates. His glitch work on this track is probably the best on Audiotool. And his experiments are always very inspirational to me. One of the truly great minds on AT.

intro: Fauko aka Pedestrian is one of the few Audiotool one man bands. His compositions are influenced mainly by the Synthpop of the 1980s whereby the pleasant timbre of his voice makes its own contribution to their authenticity and evokes memories of bands like 'Depeche Mode' and 'Camouflage'. Not only fans of the early electronic Pop music will get their money's worth.
about:
Tom Davis
Place: Currently living in the Washington, DC metro area
Age: 42
Profession: sysadmin

interview.a: Whatever comes naturally. My last couple of published tracks (last one from fauko and first one from pedestrian) could be considered Synthpop.

I sang in church as a child and throughout my youth. I also took piano lessons briefly as a child (wish I had stuck with that!) and played trumpet in school bands from age 10-18. I also played with brass ensembles at parties and events as an older teen. Oh yes, I was in a rock band for a brief period of time after graduating high school. I never studied much in terms of theory – making music was just a fun hobby for me.

In terms of attempting original composition, as a young teen when I got a Casio MT 205 keyboard as a gift. It wasn't much more than a child's toy, but this was circa 1987 and about all my parents were willing to shell out for. The best thing about it was it had an output that I was able to utilize with a jack on a boombox on which I would record to an audio cassette. I would record what amounted to accompaniment and then attempt to add a solo melody with my trumpet. That lasted until I was about 19. I stopped making music completely until discovering Audiotool many years later.
In late 2011, I was exploring the Chrome app store and ran across Audiotool. It was and remains far beyond anything I expected to find for making music online.

To the Audiotool team: Thank you for the site/DAW/community you have created and continue to develop. AT is a true gift to anyone interested making electronic music and I believe it provides value to users of any experience, from novice to pro.

To the community: It is amazing to experience the music you create and share. I have been introduced to and influenced by styles of music I never would have considered listening to before joining Audiotool. I appreciate the inspiration, encouragement guidance and friendship that I have received from you.

tracks.name: system of a system
tracks.desc: I love the concept of this track. AudioArchitect is one of the best artists I have come across when it comes to articulately expressing his state of mind through his music.

tracks.name: evolutionary humanism
tracks.desc: This track has some wonderful old school elements in it. The bass in this track is hypnotic to me. Whenever I hear this track, it generally takes about 24 hours to completely work its way through my brain.

tracks.name: Auf Kurs (2015)
tracks.desc: A recent remake by this prolific and legendary AT artist, this track showcases signature Sndbrgn sound that can be found in his work, regardless of genre; intricate percussion with mysterious synth work delicately layered on top.

tracks.name: Waiting Door
tracks.desc: Another AT legend typically known for dark Techno tracks, this track shows that Cripta is a versatile artist with outstanding synthpop sensibilities (which can also be found in the best collaboration on AT – @sandcripten ).

tracks.name: leck am reaktor
tracks.desc: The synth work in this track still haunts me (in a good way) to this day. Where the heck did this guy go?!

intro:
Since the early Audiotool days Mikel Boyles aka Nowai has been a guarantor of a sound which - whatever style he is trying his hand at - shows his unfailing feeling for the origins of that musical culture. Whether Detroit -Techno, Chicago-House or Oldschool-Jungle: his tracks could without exception have had their place on the vinyls of that era.

His portfolio is reminiscent of a visit in a records store in the 1990s.
about:
Mikel Arrid-Thomas Boyles
Place: Born in Pensacola, Florida, USA but spent most of my years in Fresno, California, USA.
Age: 26
Profession: Professional Dad

interview.a: Fluid. I constantly shift between different genres and styles of music. Repetitive Techno one day, grooving House the next, Ambient and D&B on another.

As far as playing actual instruments I didn't really start until I was a sophomore in University and that was guitar. Nowadays, aside from making tracks on Audiotool, I play guitar when the feeling strikes.
I started making really bad mashup Hip Hop tracks back in 2007. I'd literally take an instrumental track, an acapella track, and smash them together using Fruity Loops (now called FL Studio).

I discovered Hobnox Audiotool in the winter of 2009. I made a plethora of pretty basic Techno tracks and haven't looked back since. I watched it go from a pretty basic app to something that can be used to produce very impressive tracks if you have the vision and skill and a powerful enough computer!

To the community: Just have fun making music. Seriously. Don't get caught up in trying to make the next big club hit, trying to recruit everyone on the site to be your follower, or making tons of money off of your music. If you make good tracks people will find them.

To the Audiotool Devs: Thank you so much for your hard work. Because of you lot I've been able to hear some incredible music, meet some awesome people, and find a way to make the music I always wished I could! Thanks for the spotlight!

tracks.name: invaders
tracks.desc: Christian-Chrom is a more recent artist I stumbled upon. I wish I had heard his tracks earlier. He is another who likes to have fun and experiment with different synths and tools much like noraus. Another great inspiration to try mixing things up.

tracks.name: Subsonic Collision
tracks.desc: Choosing my favorite 808chunk track is like choosing which of your children you love most. 808chunk has always been a great ambassador of Oldskool music and very tight sound design. I love hearing how intricate his tracks are and how well put together they are. His versions of Detroit Techno, Acid Techno, Oldskool Tech/House, and Electro music will always have a soft spot in my heart and is something that I never tire of hearing.

tracks.name: Kiçini Bikini
tracks.desc: I don't know much about Austek other than that he is Turkish, makes incredible tracks, and loves to move between styles of music with almost every release. His tracks always sound polished and easy on the ears. I hope to improve my production values to match with his and one day I hope to be able to download one of his tracks ;)

tracks.name: Guitar
tracks.desc: 5 years ago I was absolutely floored by what yas had created. I remember my browser kept crashing whenever I just tried opening the song and playing it. This one really inspired me to try chopping samples more and is a tactic I currently use in nearly every track. yas, along with the last person in this list were very influential in my musical development.

tracks.name: Levitation
tracks.desc: The first of my two favorite Audiotool junglists. She has a way of using samples, manipulating them, and fitting them seamlessly into a track. This track in particular was such a great example of classic Jungle that it ended up on the

intro: In his past four years on Audiotool Teqtoniq has made some kind of a fairytale career. From his beginnings as a comparative novice in production to one of the most successful and inspiring artists on the website. Hardly a week goes by without one of his tracks at higher places in the charts. Whereby he plays on the claviature of contemporary electronic music as if he never did anything else before.
about:
Arjun Sharma
Place: Toronto, Ontario
Age: 18
Profession: I'm studying Biochemistry at McMaster University.

interview.a: One of the things I'm most proud of is that I feel like I'm able to work on music from a wide variety of styles and genres. I love to play around with hybrid genres, and I enjoy pulling influences from all over the place - jazz, EDM, classical, movie OSTs, whatever.

I've been classically trained in piano since I was about eight or nine, in addition to studying music theory. I've also played viola for 7 years in both small chamber groups and larger orchestras. And sometimes I play around with a guitar.

I've been listening to electronic music all my life. I guess it was only natural for me to start playing around with the idea of trying to make it myself, so I started looking at free DAW options around 2008-2010, when I fooled around on a site called Splice Music (where I first used the trance10 username).

When the original Splice Music went down around 2011, I started looking for alternatives, and the visual layout of the Audiotool app at the time was pretty similar to Splice Music's layout. I decided to try a few things out. At first I just played around with loops, but then I started to play with the Pulv and it kind of snowballed from there. I would go over to my grandparents' house and just sit upstairs for hours working on music on crappy speakers - but it just captivated me.

tracks.name: please
tracks.desc: I can't express enough how emotional this track is. It's gotten me through hard times in the past, to be completely honest.

tracks.name: On The Roads

tracks.name: Rebel.

about:
Gabriel Evangelista Adão
Place: Francisco Morato (São Paulo, Brasil)
Age: 17
Profession: Etec de Francisco Morato (school), Northern Sub Records (Label)

interview.a: Experimental Old School Music
None :) Computer Music?

In 2010 I started getting interested in electronic music, so I tried out an online application which was just clipping samples. Between 2010 and 2013 I listened to several different styles of music. I was so interested that I started listening to Deep House and researching the history of Chicago House on several sites. It was there that I came across Detroit Techno which was quite different from Techno tracks I had ever heard. Most of these were not Trance but Techno with the industrial sound of Belgium. At that time I started to feel like producing music. Until one day I listened to an 808Chunk which inspired me to finally get on with it.

In 2012 I discovered Audiotool on a website for applications and game downloads. I even tried to do a song but the Adobe Flash Player on my computer was outdated. So I asked my dad to update it because my Linux computer could not support other music applications. He was so busy that it took a long time to help me upgrade the Adobe Flash Player although he is good with computers.

Then in 2013 Flash was updated and I started making music with Audiotool. The first tracks were horrible. Audiotool is a great App! But it is very difficult for those who know nothing about music production.
Thanks for your comments on all my tracks! If you are new to Audiotool and do not know anything about music ... I have to talk to you: it's not easy but do not give up!

about:
Michael Hleck
Place: Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada
Age: 28
Profession: Following my heart :P

interview.a: I like to use lots saw waveforms and play the same 4 chords over and over again. This is mostly because I have very little understanding of music theory. But that's okay. What I can tell you, is that if this was like, some kind of music mag interview. I'd fake a european accent so I sound all inspired and underground, and say some rubbish stock line like: "With Compu'ers, it's like the possibilities are endless."

I've never really played any traditional instruments, and in fact I had no interest in music at all until I was 15. I mean, I guess you could call a computer an instrument! So, in that case I'm a real professional at pressing/clicking buttons that make sounds. So Pro, brah.

Somewhere in the early 2000's, I fumbled my way through trial and error and learned how to make tracks in early versions of Fruity Loops. Which was funny, because there were so many really bad glitches. Like, really bad glitches no one explained to me. I mostly ignored normal social interactions like meeting girls and going to parties, to invest my time into pressing a key, hearing a stupid bass sample, laughing, and repeating. It's been about 10 or so years and now I have friends to come over, press a key, hear a stupid bass sample, laugh, and repeat.

"Back in the Day" when the site was still under "The Company That Shall Not Be Named" (AT seems to hate Hobnox or something, it's basically the Voldemort of Audiotool), My friend Tyler told me about Audiotool.

I used it to mess around while I was in class, instead of doing my work. Coincidentally, I also got top marks in that class as a network engineer. Also coincidentally, no one really seems to want to hire network engineers, and so I went on to work doing security. This is where I spent the majority of my time using Audiotool, as the cities highest paid musician.

interview.a:
My style... hmm. Well I like make easy beats in general which I experiment with. But I have two main styles:

The first: Stupid Melodic House Music. Which are cheesy melodies on easy drum beats are catchy sounds.

The second is my Disco Drugs. Which are danceable tracks between 120 and 140 bpm with the classic four on the floor beats with a lot of filtered sounds and in particular cases the 303.

Also I try to make Drum 'n' Bass and other stuff... n_n

I have been listening to Disco Music since I was 10 years old and I lhave listened to a lot of different music genres. I tried to play the guitar, keyboard, drums and some percussions but my experience is practically null. I only know the basics of music production. The scale, some chords and patterns, drums ... Currently I'm trying to study a little more... n_n

Well ... when I was a kid my dad had some old instruments. I had fun over the years creating sounds which I liked. In 2008 I tested a lot of programs for making electronic music and I made some tracks for fun. Oriented in tribal beats, percussions and drums. But I stopped until I found AT. n_n

One day, searching for musical games, I found the emulator RT 909 by André Michelle and I liked a lot.

Later in 2011 I came across Audiotool and I made some tracks. But I left it again to sort out my musical idea and returned in 2012 as Universe Cosmic. n_n

interview.a: I'd describe my style as instrumental Alternative Hip Hop and sometimes I'll make trap music.

I taught myself how to play piano in fourth grade, and I've been a percussionist since the 6th grade. I'm currently playing quints in my High school marching band. (Playing Quints builds some serious chops. My wrists are a lot stronger now.)

I discovered Audiotool while looking up ways to make music for free. At first I wanted to be an animator but I found out quickly that I'm really bad at drawing and that animating is really difficult. So I found this website called Soundation and it was cool, but I had a feeling that I could find something better. I did a Google search, found Audiotool, and the rest is history.

My advice to the Audiotool community is to always be yourself, always experiment, never be intimidated by people better than you, and for the love of all that is good DON'T DELETE YOUR TRACKS. THERE WILL ALWAYS BE SOMEONE DISAPPOINTED WHEN YOU DO THAT. That's all. Stay classy Audiotool.

intro:

about:
David Thompson
Place: London, England.
Age: 14
School: Student at Dwight School London

interview.a: Dubstep music. I like making Dubstep music because there's a lot of freedom within the genre. (There are many different styles of Dubstep.) I really enjoy making melodies, with triplets and swing notes. I like it when a few good melodies overlap in the drop, it creates a really "full" sound. I never learn't music theory.. I kinda just go with what sounds good. I think it's working for me so far. :)

I play piano. I stopped having lessons about 2 years ago, now I teach myself. I used to learn guitar but it hurt my fingers too much, so I stopped. Now, my computer is my instrument. But whenever I write melodies..I always go to my piano to play it, and make chords and stuff.

2011 - 2012 i think..It was a few years ago. I first got into EDM music when I was in the car with my dad and a cool song came on the radio. It was Superbad by Doctor P and Flux Pavilion. I was like whoaaaaa. Before this time, I didn't really listen to music much. It was this track that first got me interested in the whole EDM scene. I found all kinds of music. I unearthed a passion for music within myself that I didn't even know was there. I loved music so much I wanted to see if i could create my own. So i looked on the internet for any free online music production software. And after some extensive searching i found a yahoo answers post where someone recommended audiotool.com. This is when I found Audiotool.

2011-2012. I started out making sample tracks (like most people.) I just put some samples into the timeline and published them under the alias "Lumax". Then for about half a year I just stopped. I think i got bored of that. Then one day when I was visiting a friend, he told me that he wanted to become a music producer. I told him about Audiotool, and produced a track in his house. This was my first actual track ever. "Alone" (I've deleted it now). I got back into Audiotool. I began to make Dubstep, since I was really into it. Then I made some experimental/ambient type tracks, and now I'm back into Dubstep. :)

THANK YOUUUUUU

I want to thank everyone. First of all the Devs, thanks for creating such an amazing and versatile software

I'd also like to thank all of my followers. I never imagined that I would get anywhere close to 600 followers, let alone being this week's featured artist :O. I don't always like my music that much but i'm glad you guys do.

To all the new/aspiring producers out there: DON'T GIVE UP! I didn't get to where I am by giving up. Just make what you enjoy, and become a part of the wonderful community we have here. The only way to improve with your music is to keep making it, and fine tuning your style.

Thanks for listening, guys. :)

So when I went to secondary school I played the flute... and that is pretty much my musical experience before Audiotool eheh. Not so much, really but I listened to a lot of music. When I was small my father made me listen to Peter Gabriel from whom I learned never to love only one single style.

I started making music from zero experience (oh yes the flute...) with Audiotool three years ago. Making music by my own was never in my plans but when a friend showed me his tracks on this mysterious app I was inspired. After this I decided to open an account and to publish some tracks.

Thank you to all my supporters for these three amazing years especially jinx, farcio and whitegrizzly for introducing me to the community and for teaching me a lot of things :). about:
Jack
Place: Virginia, United States
Age: 33
Profession: Voight-Kampff specialist at Tyrell Corp

interview.a: Music for driving up to Grand Marais in the autumn. My stuff is inspired by a mixture of Presbyterian theology and boreal forests. An essential part of what it means to be made in the image of God the creator is, I reckon, to have a creative capacity. And the ability to, more or less, create and design the sound of your instrument every time you work with a synthesizer is part of what makes synthesis so amazing. I dig that.

At the same time I was listening to Grieg, Beethoven, and Brahms I was playing piano in a jazz band. We used to go into the practice rooms in my old high school and listen to really off the wall stuff during study hall. The drummer in our band lent me a copy of the Richard D. James Album, and from there I spiraled into Autechre, Bochum Welt, Photek, Global Goon and have since then only fallen more deeply in love with what a synthesizer can do.

I've been composing music as long as I've been playing instruments. I played piano from the age of 6, and pretty early on was interested in improvisational jazz, but also orchestral music and big cinematic and romantic musical landscapes.

A friend of mine (who is a brilliant musician, and analog genius) put me onto audiotool back in 2010. Since then, I've made around 471 tracks...which...if you do the math...is a track every 4 days. I don't have a lot of free time, and tend to just jot down melodies as they pop into my head. But this year I've been in grad school and only published a track every few months or so.

It should blow our minds that Audiotool is possible, and that it is free. in 2014 audiotool sent Kepz, Frigolito, RNZR and me to Moogfest, and I am not exaggerating to say it was a life changing experience. I think the culture of synthesizers is an incredibly generous and beautiful one. We are all people who have a deep respect for the people who make our instruments--and have to confess that much of what we do would not be possible without the artists behind the instrument. At Moogfest last year, Tom Oberheim spoke a bit about his role in the invention of MIDI, and he paused for a moment to play music written and performed by artists. He was moved to tears at hearing what other people had made out of his instruments. Clark did something similar during his set, pausing to express amazement at what he was able to get from his machines. I think that expresses a perfect summary of the community of electronic music--it's a place where technician and artist coexist peacefully, and get to make some pretty amazing sounds together. That's why I love the fact that Audiotool is full of old and young, remix and original, collaboration and solo act. Thanks to Audiotool for creating the instruments and the place, and thanks to all you Audiotools for making the place amazing.

Just a top notch, brilliant artist.

intro: [Hybrid] has been an insider tip for a considerable time. About three years ago his deep, atmospheric track 'Please Keep Breathing' had not only made it on the second a+ compilation 'Amorfx', it also topped the a-records charts on Beatport for more than a year. His mixture of abstract beats and intense sound formations is definitely worth a listen.
about:
Kahliel Jones
Place: San Francisco, California, USA
Age: 20

interview.a: My style is a combination of samples (used as textures) that are tweaked using the pitch knob on the Machiniste, coupled with many different variations of leads and pads (primary created with the Heisenberg; occasionally the Pulv) and a wide variation of Drums and percussion, typically created through the Machiniste and Beatbox 8. I don't fall under any specific Genre, I experiment with many sounds, although I always try to create a sense of bliss and euphoria through my music, and I always try to include some type of Jazz.

I was born into a very musical family; My mother sang and played guitar, my grandfather also sang and played piano, drums and any kind of brass instrument you could think of, and I had a cousin named Ronnie Brown who used to play for a world-famous Avante Garde Jazz group named Sun Ra (lead Trumpeter). My grandfather was the one to teach me my first instrument (harmonica) and he really took it seriously whenever we'd sing songs in the car, so he gave me a better sense of Key and Tone. When I was about 11, I took my first band class and picked up the trumpet. I did learn much as far as trumpet goes, until I spent a week with my cousin Ronnie, who taught me, just whithin that week, everything I know about reading music. From there on, I played trumpet til I was about 13 and picked up the guitar. Shortly after, my mom bought me my first drumset. So essentially was learning guitar and drums at the same time, and got pretty decent at both. I still play guitar a lot, but I'm primarily producing on Audiotool now.
I started creating my own music, when I was about 13 years old. Writing lyrics and composing guitar chords to create my initial sound. over the years, my writing skills have developed tremendously.

I discovered Audiotool on a foggy morning when I was 16. me and a couple of stoners went back to a friend's house to get all lazy and surf the internet. I remember know how it got brought up but I do remember one of my girl friends saying "you know there's an app on chrome that you can make dubstep on right?". I imediately had a change in mood and asked over and over again to tell me the name of the app (Audiotool). Since then, I've been hooked. Making music and practicing every day on the app. I can almost remember my first "favorite" Who I believe was from OscarOllie (love you). Audiotool changed my entire life, and essentially gave me more purpose as a creator.

I only really have 2 things to say:

1. Thank you So much to the audiotool crew that helped make this DAW possible. I really can't tell you how much I appreciate having a free platform that still works as an authentic software to create professional production. Like I said earlier, it's changed my life in the most positive way, and now I feel like I'm at a place where I can really make a living off of it.

2. To the AT community: Follow your own path. Whether you want as many followers as possible, or just live to create a beautiful sound for your own purposes, FOLLOW YOUR OWN PATH. I'm personally trying to make it in the music industry and sometimes I have to ignore a lot of the AT users that I even look up to, just because their advice doesn't suit my path. So in the end, trust your gut, and make it whatever you want it to be. But also, PRACTICE. Practice practice practice and don't get lazy. Don't resort to only using loops that are off-key, really put time and effort into it, and understand that it is WORK and wont be easy at first; With that mentality, you wont be so overwhelmed. That's all I have to say.

tracks.name: [Dem.Naps]
